scope: Bosnia and Herzegovina tactical and coaching team_profile only
missing_fields: complete set-piece taker and target map
full recent structured sample against high-tempo opponents
notes: No match prediction, group projection, champion projection, or betting advice.

coach_profile.summary: Barbarez's Bosnia and Herzegovina project leans on experience, emotional intensity, and direct central routes. The team are more comfortable in structured, physical games than in high-speed, open-space races.

possession_structure.summary: Bosnia do not need long possession dominance. Their controlled attacks are usually there to create service into the striker, second-ball fights, or crossing situations.
possession_structure.build_up: Back four base with central midfielders trying to feed direct service or recycle toward the wings.

pressing_profile.summary: The team can press in bursts, especially from emotional momentum, but they are not best described as a full-match high-press side.

defensive_block.summary: Bosnia are more comfortable in a mid/low block where the line can stay connected and the game can be slowed down. Their biggest discomfort appears when fast opponents stretch them repeatedly.

attacking_transition.summary: Bosnia can counter when the first pass finds the striker or an aggressive runner early, but their transition ceiling is more about timing and service than raw team speed.

defensive_transition.summary: This is a real concern area. If Bosnia lose the first duel after their own attack breaks down, recovery pace and spacing can be exposed.

set_piece_attack.summary: Set pieces are one of Bosnia's clearest support weapons because they amplify experience, size, and box instincts.

set_piece_defense.summary: Usually serviceable because of size and experience, but the exact quality still depends on final lineup freshness and mobility.

game_state_behaviour.leading: When ahead, Bosnia are likely to lower tempo, defend territory, and trust experience rather than chase a second stylistic phase.
game_state_behaviour.trailing: When trailing, they can increase crossing volume and commit more bodies around the striker, but the comeback route may become predictable.

substitution_tendency.summary: Expected changes are more likely to reinforce direct service, box presence, or emotional intensity than to shift into a radically different possession model.

key_player_roles.note: Still the clearest penalty-box and hold-up reference, even if mobility is no longer the defining feature.
Important because he can attack off the main striker and raise the running output.
One of the most important pace and carrying outlets from the wide areas.
Critical for keeping the team emotionally and structurally connected.

tactical_risks: dependence on service quality into older attacking references
midfield coverage when match tempo rises
predictability when chasing a game through crosses
data_gaps: clean lineup-linked evidence of back-four versus two-forward usage
current-minute load and freshness of veteran core
recent high-quality sample against elite transition teams

confidence_notes.reason: Supported by player profiles and team tendencies.
Needs fresher sample versus stronger opposition.
Widely plausible but still needs video-backed review.

market_relevant_flags: If the game becomes cross-heavy, Bosnia become more live
If forced into repeated recovery sprints, Bosnia's defensive floor drops quickly
Veteran freshness is a major hidden variable

challenge_points: Are we over-anchoring on veteran reputation instead of current mobility?
How much does Dzeko's role still increase box threat at current age?
Can Dedic alone solve the pace gap against faster groups?
